---
title: "CoordCube insertions for brief.qmd"
---

## Insertion 1: After "spatial computation" paragraph

**Location:** After the paragraph ending "The figures above measure the consequence: HashMap degrades with data volume; the coordinate space does not."

**Insert:**

```markdown
### The second channel: CoordCube spatial queries

CoordPath treats N syllables as a linear key. The same bytes can be
reinterpreted as a D-dimensional grid (CoordCube) where each dimension
has R syllables of resolution (N = D × R). Conversion between the two
views costs 960 ps — a single assertion check — and changes no state.

This dual view enables two complementary access channels:

| Channel | Abstraction | Operation | Throughput |
|---------|------------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | CoordPath | O(1) direct array lookup | 0.39 ns |
| 1 | CoordPath | CoordSet axis AND filter | 338 Melem/s |
| 2 | CoordCube | Proximity generation (r=1) | 96 Melem/s |
| 2 | CoordCube | Proximity generation (r=5) | 353 Melem/s |
| 2 | CoordCube | Bounding box enumeration | 289-699 Melem/s |
| 2 | CoordCube | Distance metrics (hamming, euclidean, manhattan) | ~320 ps |
| 1+2 | SpatialKV | Storage-backed proximity query | 31-584 Melem/s |

{{< include ../_include/bench/fig-bench-2ch.qmd >}}

**Distance metrics cost ~320 ps** — below a single CoordSpace array lookup
(390 ps). Hamming, Euclidean approximation (Newton-Raphson sqrt, 12
iterations), and Manhattan all operate entirely on registers with no
memory access. The compiler inlines and unrolls all three to the point
where the measurement is dominated by timing infrastructure noise.

{{< include ../_include/bench/fig-bench-cubedist.qmd >}}

**Proximity throughput increases with radius.** Generating a 121-path
neighborhood (radius 5) achieves 353 Melem/s — 6.4x higher than a
single-path generation (55 Melem/s at radius 0). The fixed setup cost
(~8.6 ns for range computation) is amortized over more output paths.
The mixed-radix counter yields paths at ~1.7-9.4 ns each, with cost
decreasing as the number of paths grows.

{{< include ../_include/bench/fig-bench-cubeproximity.qmd >}}

**Dimensional scaling follows output count, not N.** Proximity time
grows with the number of generated paths (2r+1)^D, not with N directly.
Per-path cost decreases from 7.4 ns/path at D=1 to 3.3 ns/path at D=4
as setup overhead amortizes. This contrasts with CoordSpaceN tree
lookups, where cost is O(N) for a single element regardless of output
count — because the operations serve different purposes: single-element
locate vs region enumeration.

{{< include ../_include/bench/fig-bench-cubescaling.qmd >}}

**KV proximity queries separate generation from lookup.** The
`SpatialKV` trait generates paths via CoordCube then looks up each
path in the CoordSpace tree. Path generation accounts for only 5% of
total time (15 ns); the remaining 95% is tree traversal (196 ns for
9 lookups at 21.8 ns each) and Vec push (74 ns). For sparse stores
where the tree fits in L1 cache, total time drops to 47.8 ns (188
Melem/s). Empty stores measure 15.4 ns — pure generation cost.

{{< include ../_include/bench/fig-bench-kvproximity.qmd >}}

The architectural consequence: no hash-based system can replicate this
dual channel. A HashMap key cannot be reinterpreted as a spatial
coordinate. CoordPath and CoordCube share the same bytes; the choice
between linear precision and spatial power is an interpretation shift,
not a data structure change.
```

---

## Insertion 2: Update spatial query paragraph

**Location:** The line "Spatial query: CoordSet bitwise AND resolves compound axis filters at 329 Melem/s — 137x faster than HashMap scan."

**Replace with:**

```markdown
**Spatial query — two tiers:**

- Set membership: CoordSet bitwise AND resolves compound axis filters
  at 338 Melem/s — 150x faster than HashMap scan.
- Region generation: CoordCube proximity enumerates L∞ neighborhoods
  at 96-353 Melem/s; bounding boxes at 289-699 Melem/s. HashMap has
  no equivalent operation.
```

---

## Insertion 3: Application domains — spatial reasoning

**Location:** The "Graph and multi-dimensional query" paragraph.

**Replace with:**

```markdown
**Graph and multi-dimensional query.** Each node maps to a coordinate,
each edge type to a bit array. Adjacency reduces to a bitwise AND over
175 machine words — no index intersection. Beyond bitwise queries,
CoordCube adds spatial region generation over the same coordinate
space: proximity search (find nodes near a center), bounding box
enumeration (nodes within a hyper-rectangle), and distance metrics
(hamming, euclidean, manhattan). These are arithmetic operations on
the existing byte array, not index scans.
```
